Q1: My study routine was honestly a bit scattered because I don't have reliable internet at home, so I had to plan around when I could get to campus or a friend's place. I'd spend maybe three or four hours at a stretch when I did get access, trying to download everything I needed. Most of my actual studying happened at home with printed notes and audio recordings I made of lectures. I tried to keep a rhythm of studying in the evenings when my house was quieter, listening to my recordings while reviewing notes.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot based on when I could actually get online and when deadlines were approaching. Some weeks I'd be consistent, studying a bit each evening, but other weeks everything piled up and I'd have to cram. When exams were coming, I'd push harder and find ways to get to campus more often. The lack of internet at home made it impossible to have a truly regular schedule tied to the LMS.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I knew there was something specific I needed—like checking assignment instructions, downloading lecture materials, or seeing if grades were posted. Sometimes I'd log in just to make sure I hadn't missed any announcements since I couldn't check regularly. The gamified tasks they introduced actually made me curious to log in more because there was this element of earning points that felt satisfying.
Q4: Honestly, using the LMS never became a daily routine for me because of my internet situation. But when I was on campus, I'd make it a point to check everything before leaving. The gamified elements helped because I'd want to see my progress and complete challenges. It became more of a weekly intensive session rather than something I did casually every day.
Q5: The video lectures were the most useful for me because I learn best by listening. I'd watch them and sometimes record the audio on my phone so I could replay them at home without internet. The lecture recordings felt like having the professor available whenever I needed to hear an explanation again. I also found the assignment rubrics helpful because they told me exactly what was expected.
Q6: I basically ignored the discussion forums completely—I never posted anything there. With my limited internet time, I had to prioritize downloading materials over participating in online conversations. Some of the extra reading links I also skipped because I couldn't always access external websites. I focused on what would directly help me pass exams and complete assignments.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to what would be assessed or if it helped me understand a difficult concept. The gamified tasks were interesting because they made even small activities feel rewarding with points and badges. If I could see how something would help my grade or my understanding, I'd make time for it despite my constraints.
Q8: Required activities always came first because I couldn't afford to miss them with my already low attendance. Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't always afford time-wise, though the gamified ones tempted me more than regular optional content. When optional tasks had points attached through the gamification system, they felt more worthwhile. Otherwise, I'd skip them to focus on essentials.
Q9: Almost all my real studying happened offline with materials I'd downloaded or printed. I'd record lectures on my phone and listen to them repeatedly at home. I also borrowed textbooks from classmates and took handwritten notes that I reviewed constantly. My notebook was probably my most important study tool, even though the LMS would never know it existed.
Q10: There were many nights where I'd study for three or four hours at home with my audio recordings and notes, and none of that showed up anywhere in the system. I'd work through practice problems on paper, discuss concepts with my study group in person, and review my handwritten summaries. The LMS only saw me during those brief windows when I had access, not the hours of preparation happening offline.
Q11: Yes, this was basically my whole approach to the course. I'd download everything I could during one session—videos, PDFs, lecture slides—and then study them for days or weeks offline. I'd watch a video once online, then listen to my audio recording of it maybe five or six more times at home. The LMS would only register that single view, not all my subsequent engagement.
Q12: There were definitely times when the stress got to me, especially when multiple deadlines converged and I couldn't get online when I needed to. My attendance was low partly because of transportation and internet issues, which created this cycle of feeling behind. When I felt overwhelmed, my motivation dropped and even getting to campus felt exhausting. The financial pressure of having limited resources added to everything.
Q13: Absolutely—when I was tired or stressed, I'd use my precious online time less efficiently, sometimes just staring at the screen. Competing responsibilities like helping at home meant I couldn't always get to campus when I planned. The confusion of catching up after missing classes made the LMS feel overwhelming sometimes. I had to be very strategic about what I accessed because I couldn't waste time.
Q14: Sometimes I'd be watching a video but my mind would be elsewhere, worrying about other things or just exhausted. I'd scroll through materials without really absorbing them, just trying to feel like I was doing something productive. There were times I accessed readings but couldn't focus enough to actually learn from them. The stress made it hard to be mentally present even when I was technically engaged.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts entirely because they felt less important than assignments and exams. Sometimes I'd avoid starting a difficult assignment because I was anxious about not understanding it well enough. I knew participation mattered, but with limited time and energy, I had to make hard choices. The things I avoided were usually what felt least connected to my final grade.
Q16: Deadlines were everything for me—they determined when I'd make the extra effort to get online. I'd plan my campus visits around submission dates and try to complete everything in those sessions. The pressure of deadlines actually helped me focus and prioritize despite my constraints. Without deadlines, I might have let things slide even more given my low motivation.
Q17: When I got good feedback or saw my high exam scores, it reminded me that my approach was working despite the obstacles. Positive feedback motivated me to keep pushing through the stress. The gamified task feedback was immediate and satisfying, which helped during the course. Knowing I was performing well academically helped balance out my anxiety about attendance and participation.
Q18: The course structure with clear deadlines helped me plan my limited online sessions effectively. Having materials available for download was crucial for someone in my situation. The gamified elements added some excitement that made me want to engage more when I could. However, the expectation of regular online participation didn't fit well with my reality.
Q19: The ability to download materials made engagement possible for me—without that, I would have struggled much more. The gamification made logging in feel more rewarding and less like a chore. What made things harder was any expectation of synchronous participation or frequent online presence. Clear assignment instructions helped because I couldn't easily ask questions when I was studying offline.
Q20: I think some students might be exploring and trying to understand the platform or materials without being ready to submit work. They could be dealing with personal issues that make completing assignments hard even when they're trying to engage. Some might be anxious about submitting imperfect work, so they keep reviewing without finalizing anything. The activity might also include a lot of time spent confused or looking for help.
Q21: This actually describes me pretty well—my LMS activity was limited but my exam and assignment scores were high. I did most of my learning offline with downloaded materials and personal study methods. Someone could be very engaged with the content without that engagement being visible online. The LMS doesn't capture studying from printed notes, listening to recordings, or discussing with classmates in person.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show when someone accessed materials and submitted work, which gives a basic picture of participation. They can track patterns like login frequency and time spent on videos. But they completely miss offline studying, the quality of engagement during online sessions, and all the learning that happens through other means. They can't measure understanding, effort, or the obstacles someone overcame to engage.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely trying to understand the material and caring about learning, not just going through the motions. For me, it's about actively listening to lectures, working through problems, and connecting concepts even when it's difficult. It's showing up mentally even when you can't always show up physically or digitally. Engagement is internal commitment that might or might not be visible to anyone else.
Q24: The LMS would never show how many hours I spent listening to audio recordings at home or reviewing handwritten notes. It wouldn't capture the stress I managed while still performing well, or the extra effort required to work around not having internet. My peer interactions happened mostly in person, not online, so those 21 interactions were probably just a fraction of my actual collaboration. The data misses my resilience and the creative ways I adapted to succeed despite my constraints.
